title: The Fullest Vault
passage:
For years the case for a crop vault has been made by counting jars on the shelf. The more distinct seed samples — accessions — a genebank takes in, the more of the world's crop diversity it holds against the day some variety vanishes from the fields, and so the pitch to any collector runs one way only: gather more. Every accession banked is a variety insured, the argument goes, and a fuller vault is a safer one. It is a tidy story, and it has set the collecting targets that genebanks measure themselves against. What the story mostly skips is the plain fact that a seed on a shelf is not the same thing as a seed that will grow.

That fact was put to a working collection. At the Almvik seed store, a team led by the seed biologist Ingrid Sahlberg tracked a decade of intake, holding the species and the cold-room conditions steady but varying one thing: whether a section of the store was allowed to grow faster than the team could regenerate its seeds — grow them out to a fresh crop and bank them again. On the count everyone watches, the result was just as advertised. Each consignment of accessions brought in genuinely new varieties, landraces the store had not held before, and it did so right across the range they tracked, in the sections that kept pace with regeneration and the sections that outran it alike. Measured by distinct varieties taken in, more collecting was simply more diversity.

Then Sahlberg's team stopped counting jars and started opening them. Seeds do not keep forever, even cold and dry; every accession slowly loses the power to germinate, and well before it dies it must be grown out to a new crop — regenerated — and banked afresh. A store can regenerate only so many accessions a season, plots and hands being finite, so the larger a collection grows, the longer any one accession waits its turn. In the sections that stayed within that regeneration capacity, the share of accessions still able to germinate when pulled barely stirred, even where the collection had grown large. In the sections that outran it, the living share edged down slowly at first and then, once the collection passed about four times what one season could regenerate, fell sharply. Each further accession simply waited longer than its seeds could survive, and a variety that dies in the freezer must be hunted through the fields again, if it can be found at all — handing back the very diversity the banking had secured. Count the jars, and the safest vault is the fullest. Open them, and it is not.

This is not how the case is usually put. Tomas Renner, who runs the regional collecting network that feeds the store, puts it with no such hedging. "Take in every variety you can lay hands on — always, whatever the shelf already holds," he told me. "There is no such thing as too many accessions. If seeds die in the cold room, you regenerate them; you do not stop collecting." Sahlberg is more guarded. Regeneration capacity can indeed be enlarged, she agrees, but only so far and never cheaply: the rarest accessions are the slowest and dearest to grow out, and no store she knows of can regenerate its whole holding fast enough to bank without limit.

The decade's record, read whole, is more divided than Renner's rule allows. It does not show that banking seed is a false economy — the varieties saved are real, and where regeneration keeps pace they run all the way up. What it shows is that "more accessions" and "more diversity saved" hold together only while the store can keep alive what it takes in; past that point, in the sections that outrun their own regeneration, the dead jars quietly cancel the gain. The number that matters, in the end, is not how many accessions a vault holds. It is how many it can keep alive. A collection matched to what it can regenerate can take in almost any amount of seed. One that outruns it has a ceiling, and it sits lower than the collecting targets like to admit.
— Petra Lindholm

accession = a distinct seed sample held in a genebank collection
regeneration = growing a stored seed sample out to a fresh crop to renew its seed stock

questions.prompt: What is this text mainly about?

options.text: That every seed sample a genebank banks is one more crop variety insured against being lost from the fields.
That banking seed saves real crop diversity, but only stays the safer insurance while the store can keep its accessions alive.
That genebanks should give priority to the crop varieties most at risk of vanishing from farmers' fields before taking in others.
That seeds gradually lose the power to germinate in storage and so must be grown out to a fresh crop from time to time.

questions.rationale: Family ELF-TYPE-004 (main idea / whole-text gist), edge position 1. WHY-KEY (B): the arc runs phenomenon (a fuller vault pitched as straightforwardly safer) -> evidence (Almvik's decade of matched intake; each consignment adds genuinely new varieties 'right across the range they tracked') -> complication (in the sections that outran regeneration, the living share 'fell sharply' past about four times a season's capacity, and dead accessions 'handing back the very diversity the banking had secured') -> verdict, paragraph 5: 'more accessions and more diversity saved hold together only while the store can keep alive what it takes in ... The number that matters, in the end, is not how many accessions a vault holds. It is how many it can keep alive.' B paraphrases that bounded verdict without lifting a distinctive four-word run. WHY-DISTRACTORS: A = scope_error -- promotes the opening pitch ('Every accession banked is a variety insured, ... a fuller vault is a safer one') to the thesis; true as the claim the passage sets out to qualify, not its point. C = surface_word_match / outside_knowledge -- recycles the 'at risk / vanishing from the fields' vocabulary into a prioritization policy the text never issues; the passage weighs the cost of regeneration but never tells genebanks which varieties to take first. D = detail-as-main / wrong_location -- accurately reports the paragraph-3 storage-decay mechanism ('every accession slowly loses the power to germinate ... it must be grown out') but that is a sub-point inside the complication, never the whole-text focus; tempting because it is a concrete, memorable detail.

questions.prompt: What does the text say about the new varieties taken in as the collection grew?

options.text: Each consignment kept bringing in genuinely new varieties, in the sections that kept pace with regeneration and those that outran it alike.
Each fresh consignment kept adding new varieties, and it would have gone on doing so without any limit however large the collection grew.
New varieties were added only in the sections that kept pace with regeneration, not in those that outran their capacity.
The new varieties came mostly from old farm landraces gathered abroad rather than from wild relatives of the crops.

questions.rationale: Family ELF-TYPE-001 (direct detail retrieval), local order. WHY-KEY (A): paragraph 2 states that each consignment 'brought in genuinely new varieties ... right across the range they tracked, in the sections that kept pace with regeneration and the sections that outran it alike.' A paraphrases both the fact (genuinely new varieties every time) and its stream-independence. WHY-DISTRACTORS: B = quantifier_upgrade (the engineered dominant trap) -- keeps the real fact but absolutizes the scope, turning 'right across the range they tracked' into 'without any limit however large the collection grew'; the passage claims the gain only within the observed range, not beyond it. C = wrong_location / cross-arm swap -- the kept-pace-versus-outran split belongs to the living share (paragraph 3), not to intake, which added new varieties in both kinds of section 'alike'; tempting because that split is the passage's central distinction. D = outside_knowledge -- supplies a plausible-sounding provenance ('gathered abroad ... rather than from wild relatives') the passage never states; the text names landraces the store had not held before but nothing about where they were collected.

questions.prompt: According to the text, what happened to the share of living accessions in the sections where the collection outran its regeneration capacity?

options.text: It barely changed at all, holding almost perfectly steady even where the collection had grown to its very largest.
It collapsed the moment the collection first grew faster than the team could regenerate its seeds.
It slipped slowly at low sizes but fell sharply once the collection passed about four times what one season could regenerate.
It recovered over the years as the staff gradually learned to test and regrow the oldest and weakest accessions ahead of the rest.

questions.rationale: Family ELF-TYPE-001 (direct detail retrieval), local order. STEM-LAW: the stem names the setup (the sections that outran regeneration capacity) but not the direction -- the living share could in principle have stayed flat, spiked at once, or recovered, so the answer is a reported empirical shape. WHY-KEY (C): paragraph 3 states that in the outrunning sections the living share 'edged down slowly at first and then, once the collection passed about four times what one season could regenerate, fell sharply.' C paraphrases that two-stage curve and its roughly-fourfold hinge. WHY-DISTRACTORS: A = wrong_location / cross-arm swap -- 'barely changed ... even where the collection had grown to its very largest' is what the passage reports for the sections that stayed WITHIN regeneration capacity ('the share ... barely stirred'), not the outrunning ones the stem asks about; tempting because the sentence is genuinely in the text. B = quantifier_upgrade / time-shift -- overshoots 'edged down slowly at first' into an immediate collapse 'the moment the collection first grew faster'; tempting to a reader who remembers only that the outrunning sections went bad. D = outside_knowledge -- a plausible operational improvement (triaging the oldest accessions) the passage never reports happening; tempting because regeneration and testing are named in the passage.

questions.prompt: What does the text imply about why the capacity-matched sections kept their seeds alive at large size while the outrunning sections did not?

options.text: Because the capacity-matched sections happened to hold hardier crop species whose seeds survive far longer in cold storage.
Because stored seed can never be kept alive at all unless every accession is grown out again each single season.
Because seeds must be grown out to a fresh crop from time to time before they lose the power to germinate.
Because it is the length of the wait for regeneration, not the collection's size in itself, that kills an accession.

questions.rationale: Family ELF-TYPE-002 (inference / implication), one logical inch beyond the text. WHY-KEY (D): paragraph 3 reports that 'the larger a collection grows, the longer any one accession waits its turn', that within capacity the living share 'barely stirred, even where the collection had grown large', and that in the outrunning sections 'each further accession simply waited longer than its seeds could survive'. The one-inch inference is that the killer is the waiting time, not size as such -- a large collection that keeps its wait short stays alive, so D states exactly the passage's implied mechanism without overstating. WHY-DISTRACTORS: A = wrong mechanism / contradicted -- the study held 'the species ... steady' across sections, so the capacity-matched ones did not hold hardier species; tempting because longer-storing seed sounds like a natural explanation. B = quantifier_upgrade / too_far -- inflates the bounded finding into the absolute 'can never be kept alive ... unless ... each single season', which contradicts both the capacity-matched sections that stayed alive without yearly regrowing and Sahlberg's view that capacity 'can indeed be enlarged ... but only so far'. C = too_literal -- restates the surface rule that seeds 'must be grown out ... before they lose the power to germinate', but that rule holds in every section and so does not explain why only the outrunning ones lost seeds; it names the requirement, not the size difference the question asks about.

questions.prompt: What is the writer's attitude toward Renner's claim that a vault can never hold too many accessions?

options.text: Broadly persuaded: she largely accepts Renner's rule and treats the seed deaths as a minor gap that more regeneration plots will close.
Qualified: she grants that banking saves real diversity but holds that maxing out accessions backfires once a store outruns its regeneration.
Convinced: she shares Renner's certainty that a vault simply cannot take in too much seed, whatever it already holds.
Dismissive: she decides that the diversity a vault preserves is too small to be worth the trouble of collecting.

questions.rationale: Family ELF-TYPE-005 (stance / tone / attitude), edge position 5; signature trap is attribution swap. Hedge-balanced by design: options A and B are BOTH cautious/qualified in form, so the item cannot be solved by the 'pick the only hedged option among absolutes' structural tell -- the discriminator is direction and content, not hedge form. WHY-KEY (B): Renner states the absolute rule ('Take in every variety you can lay hands on -- always ... There is no such thing as too many accessions'), but the writer answers in paragraph 5 that 'The decade's record, read whole, is more divided than Renner's rule allows', granting that 'the varieties saved are real' while insisting that 'more accessions and more diversity saved hold together only while the store can keep alive what it takes in' and that a collection that outruns its regeneration 'has a ceiling'. 'Qualified' names exactly this real-but-bounded position -- the diversity gain accepted, the absolute rejected. WHY-DISTRACTORS: A = direction reversal, hedged in form (the working-shape trap) -- it is just as cautious-sounding as the key ('broadly', 'largely'), but it lands the writer on Renner's side and downgrades the losses to 'a minor gap that more regeneration plots will close'; the passage does the opposite, treating the dead jars as the binding ceiling and noting that 'no store she knows of can regenerate its whole holding fast enough to bank without limit'. C = role_or_attribution_swap (the signature TYPE-005 trap) -- assigns Renner's quoted certainty to the writer; tempting because his 'always, whatever the shelf already holds' line is the most emphatic stance in the passage. D = polarity overshoot -- pushes the writer's reservation into outright dismissal, which the concession that 'the varieties saved are real, and where regeneration keeps pace they run all the way up' plainly contradicts.

fictional_entities: Ingrid Sahlberg, seed biologist and study lead (invented; all findings invented)
Tomas Renner, director of a regional seed-collecting network (invented; all quotes invented)
generator_meta.question_geometry: Q1 main idea (edge, pos1) / Q2 detail (intake coverage) / Q3 detail-comparison (STEM-LAW: stem names the outrunning sections, direction is a reported outcome) / Q4 inference (waiting-time-not-size mechanism) / Q5 stance (edge, pos5, HEDGE-BALANCED)
generator_meta.key_letters: B, A, C, D, B -- all four letters used, no monotonic run and no positional/alternation tell; keys mix confident-specific (Q2 intake-both-arms, Q3 two-stage curve, Q4 mechanism) with bounded/qualified (Q1 verdict, Q5 'qualified') so 'pick the hedged answer' does not score the unit blind
generator_meta.engineered_traps: quantifier_upgrade at Q2/B ('across the range tracked' -> 'without any limit however large') and Q4/B ('capacity can be enlarged only so far' -> 'can never be kept alive ... each single season'); cross-arm swap at Q2/C and Q3/A (the capacity-matched flat living-share curve offered for the outrunning sections); attribution_swap at Q5/C (Renner's absolute assigned to the writer)
generator_meta.length_tell_discipline: The key is the single longest option in NONE of the five questions (Q1 longest is D distractor; Q2 longest is B distractor; Q3 longest is D distractor; Q4 longest is C distractor; Q5 longest is A distractor). option_length_ratio per q well under the 2.36 cap.
generator_meta.stance_item_fix: Q5 uses the batch-5-proven two-hedge shape. Both A ('Broadly persuaded') and B (key, 'Qualified') are cautious in form, so no 'lone hedge among absolutes' structural leak survives; the item is decided by direction/content only -- does the writer treat the seed deaths as a minor fixable gap (A, siding with Renner) or as the binding ceiling (B, the passage's actual verdict). C and D are the absolutes (attribution-swap 'Convinced', polarity-overshoot 'Dismissive').
generator_meta.schema_note: Emits 5 questions per the long_passage_5q blueprint (ELF long passages are invariantly 5q); this intentionally exceeds candidate-item.schema.json questions.maxItems=4, which was authored for single-question seeded eval items. All other schema constraints pass as-is.
generator_meta.self_blind_solve: Solved all five from the passage alone before consulting keys: Q1=B, Q2=A, Q3=C, Q4=D, Q5=B (5/5 match). No item blind-solved to two defensible answers; the Q5 two-hedge design was verified to require reading the paragraph-5 ceiling verdict to separate B from A, and the Q4 near-two-way (D vs C) closes because C's rule holds in every section and so cannot explain the size-dependent difference.
